Contents

WHO FrameworkScoring MethodologyData SourcesLimitations & Caveats

Methodology

Understanding our approach to measuring and comparing African health systems

WHO 6 Building Blocks Framework

Our assessment is structured around the World Health Organization's six building blocks of a health system. This framework provides a comprehensive approach to understanding and evaluating health systems globally, enabling meaningful comparisons across countries with different contexts and priorities.

Service Delivery

Delivery of effective, safe, quality health services to those who need them.

Health Workforce

Health workers who are responsive, fair and efficient to achieve the best health outcomes.

Health Information Systems

Production, analysis, dissemination and use of reliable and timely health information.

Health Financing

Raising adequate funds so people can use needed services and are protected from financial hardship.

Medical Products & Technologies

Equitable access to essential medical products, vaccines and technologies.

Leadership & Governance

Strategic policy frameworks combined with effective oversight and accountability.

Scoring Methodology

Each building block is scored on a 0–100 scale based on a composite of multiple indicators. The overall country score is the arithmetic mean of all six building block scores.

70+

Strong

Meets or exceeds WHO benchmarks

50+

Moderate

Progressing but gaps remain

<50

Weak

Significant challenges ahead

Data Sources

World Health Organization (WHO)

Global Health Observatory data repository

World Bank

World Development Indicators and health expenditure data

African Union

Africa Health Strategy and institutional reports

UNICEF

Maternal and child health indicators

Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME)

Global Burden of Disease study

Limitations & Caveats

  • •Data availability varies significantly across countries and indicators
  • •Some indicators use proxy measures where direct data is unavailable
  • •Scores reflect the most recently available data, which may span different years
  • •Composite scoring inherently simplifies complex health system dynamics
